sentences.text: Well, concerning the survivors of Budapest, that is the-- that is the-- this old wives' story about Raoul Wallenberg who was introduced to-- introduced first in Hungary, to-- whom I could never find out.
But there is a book written by Levai, Eugene Levai, a Hungarian-Jewish-- Jewish writer.
He wrote a book about that.
And all the stories, the Jews by the tens of thousands of them were saved by Raoul Wallenberg.
And then the story got into the United States, and the whole scores of books, newspapers, and magazine articles, and I, I, I have file here.
It may be a six-inch, six-inch thick, all about Wallenberg, newspaper clippings and so on and so forth.

What this Wallenberg did to the Jews but-- back in Hungary, in my, in my research for my book that I wrote, and I have a chapter about the Wallenberg case, I tried to find a one single person who would have-- who would have state definitely, consciously, that Raoul Wallenberg saved me.
In the way as it's written in the book.
Or I tried to find one single copy of the, of the schutzpass, as-- as they called it, they called it in-- in German, they gave to Wallenberg, so-called Wallenberg passport, that saved.
One.
I looked all over Budapest, [INAUDIBLE], Jewish people.
Nobody could produce me one.
But my god.
There should be one at least who saved it.
At least one saved it as souvenir that is what saved my life.
Not one single, single copy of the schutzpass, because it was all, all just a-- just a mish-mash.

And I-- then I wrote in my book, and to keep telling people the story how he got there on the story that I already told, how he was, he was sent in, out, from-- from Sweden, through Germany, through Germany, into Switzerland, to, to, to Dulles, who was the head of the American Secret Service in the Second World War.

There he was briefed, and was given what was sent into Hungary, precisely with this task, of, of, of planning out and convince, convince the Hungarian political and, and military, military leadership in Hungary.
And the Germans in Hungary, a message to Hitler through Hungary.

Let Hitler withdraw his troops from Hungary, pretending that they are retreating ahead of the American army, and the American army moves into Hungary, occupy Hungary, and there be no reason for the Red Army to move further than the, than the, than the Balkans, because in the west, the Western-- Westerm capitalist, and-- and the, and the Chamberlain gang and so on, they were determined, including the British royal family who financed Hitler and the Wallenberg family who financed Hitler very, very heavily.
All that gold that you are hearing there today.
It went into German, the Germans, Germans shipped it out, out to-- to Switzerland.
And from-- to-- to Sweden.
From Sweden, they shipped it back, in the form of money and artist back to, back to Hitler, to, to, to arm-- arm Hitler.
And it's a problem even--even today.
And the army was the, the-- who was, who was, who was a scoundrel, otherwise, George Patton.
And Eisenhower was the, the commander of the unified Western allies army.
And he was so fed up with the-- when he heard this plot, because this was, was done all behind him, because the Americans had the SOS, special operation.
I don't know what.
But within that secret organization of the-- of the Americans, like the British had that the Jews served in.
And the CIA was operating there, even without or the consent of the president of the United States of America.
So when Eisenhower got wind of it, what, what's this plot?
Hear that they want to, that they want to send in, in George Patton with his army what is commanding in Italy, into Hungary.
Well, he was stunned.
Nobody ordered.
Nobody know.
Nobody authorized it, and so-- and Eisenhower demoted George Patton from general to colonel, to-- from an army commander to a, to, to a division, division commander.
And this whole plot fell, fell, fell flat.
Yeah.
Well, since it's straightforward question, stay straightforward answer, answer.
The man was Gyorgy Egri.
Originally, I find out later, when I was, I was back, back in Hungary.
But first, to tell you how, how I met with the man.
Well, I recognized that this man is in Toronto, and I recognize him as a planted agent, an informer, in [INAUDIBLE] prison when I was, I was kept prisoner in a Hungarian counterintelligence.
And the Gestapo had this headquarter in the same same military, military building.
Well, there were all kinds of activities in those years, in the late '50s, and then in the '60s, the peace movement, especially the peace movement, worldwide, worldwide movement.
And in Toronto here, we had peace demonstrations almost every week, and meetings and so on.

And at-- at one time in, in, in-- at the time, at the time, the Vietnam War, the peace movement here in Toronto called a meeting and a-- a-- a smaller meeting in the King Edward hotel on King-- on King Street, where Dr.
James G.
Endicott, the leader of the Canadian peace movement, had a report from, from Vietnam and, and, and Korea, how the Americans used poison gas and, and [INAUDIBLE] bombs against the people there.
And he's going-- he's going to give, give a report in that meeting.
And I, myself, I, myself, served, served, served for, for this movement and so on security.
When we had visitors like dean of Canterbury from England, Paul Robeson from the United States.
And Dr.
Endicott, the leader of the peace movement, [INAUDIBLE] from-- the leader of the Communist party.
So because of my war service and then the experience, I organized the security it needed.
[INAUDIBLE] was after these people, the right-winger and fascist and so.

And at this-- at this meeting, I was there, very first one, to look around the building and place people, you see, where to, where to [INAUDIBLE] and secure the, what you call them, the safety of the Dr.
Endicott.
And this, this [?
Wiesel ?] man showed up with a-- with a armful of-- armful of leaflets.
And then they started to come, and apparently two Ukrainians came, from Ukrainian organization, who were in my, in my group.
We were in a security, what I told you, go inside, put your coat and hat-- hat on, and so.
And this man, who then turned out to be that I recognized later, handed a leaflet to them.
Ooh la la, I didn't pay any attention, because they-- in those demonstrations and meetings, they were all kinds [INAUDIBLE].
There's always somebody that I don't know from the peace movement.
And then there started to be people, started to come, all within a few minutes.
And they handed leaflets.
And I stepped over to him, and asked what are leaflet.
When I looked at, right with his big fat black prints, 'Most Were Dopes.' They're all these J.G.
Endicott.
And the whole, whole, whole peace movement is just a front for the-- for the-- for the Moscow communist.
And then without, without any thinking, I just grabbed the leaflets in his arms, threw it in the, in the garbage can, that there was a garbage can right at the entrance to the, to the, to the hall.
And pardon my language, somehow I shook him, turned him around, and gave him a real kick in between the legs, as, as, as I was trained to do in my service.
And so, and he almost fell on his nose, and some that had just heard his what you call them, and just whining and just walked out from the--
No, but then it came, came back to me.
Took, took some months before somehow-- it's human nature.
And, and, and my-- my tragedies, I have always wake up early in the morning, you see, and start to begin thinking, without, without knowing or, or, or one thing.
And all of a sudden, a picture came back to me, that this man that, that, that I really flattened out, well, I know this man from somewhere.
And slowly, slowly, my god, this goes, goes back to the wartime.

And by the time it was going on for months, he was the editor of the, of the But he printed it in another place, and we had Hungarian prints up on Wilson Avenue, just, just west of of Bathurst, a small Hungarian and so, where they typed and then set on paper.
But he also had the printing there where he printed some kind of leaflets or invitations.
So, and at one day I was there reading, they're reading the proofs in the [INAUDIBLE].
And so I came to something in-- in my paper, and he couldn't think.
Just looked up, to think, what-- how should I correct it.
And when I look up, there was a, a doorway in between that editor room and the shop.
A man is standing there, and wide open eyes, wide open.
There and just looking at-- and when I looked up, and you know, when you look, what you call a glimpse with another person, you can't turn your head away right away, right away.
But then, he just looked.
He got a shock, turned back, and back to the shop.
And by the time, by the time he got out through the back door, this is [INAUDIBLE].
Whoo.
It came back so, so, so sudden.
And it was him.
And, and, and-- and in-- and in, in peace demonstrations then, and so I remember seeing the guy with [INAUDIBLE].
They were always there, the counter, there was Hungarian nationalist and Lithuanians and Polish and so against the peace movement.
And in Queens Park.
We went on, on fights with them.
You know, the signs that we carry, and so and with the stick, bzz, bing, bang each other.
And we came out because we had, we had a real majority.
No, no.
I, I, I didn't know, I didn't know what to, what to do with the man.
And then came, then came another event what we're waiting and waiting.
It came way up in 1985.
1985, the time came.
That was the time when the Tories had the nationwide campaign, and that was the year when the [INAUDIBLE] government got in.
And that was in the Hungarian house.
You know, on, on, on the Hungarian house is on, on St.
Clair Avenue.
It used to be a huge, huge synagogue, the Hungarian nationalist.
But that [INAUDIBLE], so Hungarian house.
And there was this Tory meeting there.
And I read, then, a couple days later, in the Hungarian right-wing paper, reporting about this meeting, because they were-- the editor of the paper was also a war criminal.
And never been exposed yet.
But-- but he died already.
But there are others here who are even bigger than [INAUDIBLE].

Even that, I wish I could-- but anyway, I wrote a report in, in my, in my paper that such and such a meeting took place in the Hungarian nationalist home, in the nationalist home, and who do I see among the names on the platform?
Even from Winnipeg, they, they, they imported some kind of real outright rabid Polish nationalist.
This kind of people.
And I named Hungarians that are outright the Arrow Cross party, ex-Arrow Cross members.
And among them, sitting, Egri Gyorgy.
How come-- this is how I place it.
How come, how come a Jew, probably the survivor of the Holocaust in Hungary, sitting among the most rabid right-wing politicians in, in, in the platform in a Hungarian, Hungarian house?
And a couple days later, after this news appeared in my paper, the I got a-- I got a telephone call from, from, from a lawyer.
I'm representing Mr.
Gyorgy Egri, said the man.
And he was also Hungarian, the lawyer.
And so, and so that you slandered him, and, and so did Mr.
Egri, and so on.
And I want to-- want to speak to you about it.
And then so when we had a talk and so on, but he didn't tell me then.
And only maybe a, a, a week later, I got a telephone, a telephone call from him.
So anyway, he told me this is a libel.
This is a libel.
And he, and he sued you, and so on.
And about a week later, I had a telephone, telephone call from him, that I was-- I was able, able to arrange with Mr.
Egri that you make-- you make a compromise, that you, you publish a retract of the, of the statement that you made.
And then later on, I found out from, from, from somebody that this lawyer explained to Egri [INAUDIBLE], well, look, George.
This man didn't call you a fascist right-- only said that you are mixed up, how come that you are mixed up with fascist?
So I can't make a case of it.
And that's how he happened.
And then, and then I had to accept, accept that I will write a retract.
And it gave me a chance to repeat that this is the thing that-- that I-- I excuse, that we have to retract, but it became a laughing stock, you see.
And then he came to the, came to the house, came to the house, who was a woman, Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE], the manager, manager of a paper in the town on the-- on the [INAUDIBLE] Drive, and I went there and Egri came to the house.
That close.
Well, I-- we wanted, we arranged through, to the lawyer, he tells us personally, because the lawyer, lawyer arranged it, it has to be done personally, face to face.
All right, Egri come to the house, come to the-- come to the [INAUDIBLE], and so on.
And what you want to retract and so on?
That's what he told to retract, that my accusation was not true And so on.
It was a half-baked nothing.
And sitting face to face with the men, had the same table on one end, me, and the other end, Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE], Mr.
[INAUDIBLE] sitting the same kitchen, kitchen table.

And I couldn't do nothing, because I already know, already knew, that this kind of thing so hot to stuff that I can't point a finger, finger at the-- but when I came to my, came to my book, I went to, went to a lawyer, who was a lawyer about your age, a Jewish lady, for advice.
And she told me, look, you can't-- you can't name me.
Said, it might be, and you might be lucky, and I'm almost sure it might be, even could be arranged to have a Jewish judge.

And you might be able what you are saying, that without lawyer, you are ready to stand up relying on your own prestige, that you were an officer in such and such a service, and without lawyer, I make a statement and-- and charge him.
Said, the judge might even accept, but we'll tell the court, you-- we believe you.
But prove it.
Because this is the law.
And the judge might even be, I believe you, honestly.
But I need proof.
This is how it was.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE].
And so he's still running around in the [INAUDIBLE] he's backing-- oh, when he was leaving the house, he was leaving the house, at the [INAUDIBLE], and I escorted him out.
And when he was at the door and put his hand on, I said, Egri, how's Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE]?
And the man got a shock.
But he stepped out of the door and start running.
But you see, I was well-trained.
And I indicated to [INAUDIBLE] come, come.
And-- and shooting up a half a block away, there was a car waiting for him, because we could have finished the guy off.
And all I needed, even today, [INAUDIBLE] he lose his head.
But I had to keep my cool.
I can't throw it away, because there might be comes a chance where I really can finish off the son of a bitch.
Yeah, but all, all right.
Well, it's been-- you're welcome.
And--
Well, she's Hannah Szenes, the girl that I wrote about, the girl who was my-- my girlfriend, and finally my-- my fiance, and so.
And this picture what is in front of me is in Palestine, the day when she arrived there.
And when the first time when I met her on the streets of Cairo, she was in full, full military uniform.
That's how I got to know her, and the last time when I saw her, it was in Cairo.
We didn't even have a chance to say goodbye, because our service was such on an order in two hours' time you had, you were on your way, on your-- on your mission.
Well, this picture that I'm facing and you are facing here, it is me in a full officer's dress uniform.
And this picture was taken back my hometown in Leduc, where my family was living at the time when I returned from the war.
And it was-- this picture was taken just outside of the house of my father there, in Leduc, Alberta.
Yes.
Man named Andrew Durovecz.
